input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation is grounded in Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML used for formal system description. Functional architecture is defined with Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML Image‑Based Diagram (IBD). Behavioral specification can be expanded with MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state‑machines. Simulation leveraging MathWorks Simulink, additional simulation tools, or physics‑based engines can be integrated to generate dynamic behavior, while executable code can be written in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. The approach aligns with multi‑level abstraction methodologies like SMArDT, RFLP, OOSEM, and SPES that support requirements, functions, logic, and physics modeling.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates domain‑specific computational models and data structures derived from Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE).  A SysML component diagram captures the hierarchical component‑and‑connector structure, with interface ports and connectors formalised in the SysML IBD notation.  Behavioural dynamics are represented either by MontiArc automata or by SysML v1.7 state‑machine fragments; these are translated into code or Simulink blocks for continuous‑time simulation.  Real‑time telemetry from the physical plant is stored as contextual data traces, which are aggregated in the Digital Shadow reference model (Fig. 1) and supplied as live input streams to the simulation.  Model relationships are defined via explicit connector bindings in SysML and MontiArc, with mapping rules that correlate telemetric input ports to Simulink signals or MontiArc input ports, thereby synchronising state between the digital and physical artefacts.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Digital Twin development follows a multi‑layered MBSE lifecycle, beginning with high‑level requirements, progressing through functional architecture, logical architecture, and finally the product/technical implementation. At each layer the twin is represented in increasingly detailed models: requirements are captured in natural language and requirement tables; functional architecture is expressed in SysML Requirements diagrams and block definition diagrams; logical architecture uses SysML IBDs or ADLs such as ACME or AADL to describe component‑connector structure; the product/technical layer is modeled with simulation primitives in tools like MathWorks Simulink or MontiArc automata. Transition between layers is managed by traceability links and model transformation rules, ensuring that each abstraction level is consistently derived from the preceding one. Lifecycle governance is typically performed through a model management system that enforces version control, change request workflows, and compliance checks against the MBSE methodology.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin employs SysML v1.7 for architecture modeling, with component‑connector descriptions in SysML IBDs, block definition diagrams and requirement diagrams. For logical and functional architecture, ACME, AADL, and MontiArc are also used as ADL standards. Simulation behavior is implemented using MathWorks Simulink, which follows the Simulink modeling standard.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
